db.mysql.composite.panel.title: Leftmost Prefix Rule:
db.mysql.composite.panel.desc: INDEX(city, age, name) supports: (city), (city, age), (city, age, name). NOT (age), NOT (age, name), NOT (name).
db.mongodb.compound.panel.title: Prefix Rule:
db.mongodb.compound.panel.desc: Index {city:1, age:1} supports: {city}, {city, age}, {city}.sort({age}). NOT {age} alone.
db.redis.secondary.panel.title: Pattern:
db.redis.secondary.panel.desc: Store primary data in HASH (user:*). Create ZSET indexes with score=field_value, member=user:id. Query via ZRANGEBYSCORE → get user IDs → HGETALL for each.

mysql.name: MySQL
mysql.modes.btree.label: B-Tree Index
mysql.modes.btree.desc: B+Tree traversal: root → internal nodes → leaf → data page. Index narrows search from O(n) to O(log n). Compare with Full Table Scan to see the difference.
mysql.modes.btree.principles: B+Tree keeps all data in leaf nodes; internal nodes store only keys and pointers for navigation
Tree stays balanced after every INSERT/DELETE via page splits and merges, guaranteeing O(log n) lookups
Leaf nodes are linked in a doubly-linked list, enabling efficient range scans without backtracking
InnoDB clusters rows by primary key (clustered index); secondary indexes store PK values, not row pointers
Page size (16 KB default) determines fan-out: higher fan-out = fewer levels = fewer disk reads

mysql.modes.btree.concepts.definition: Smallest I/O unit (16 KB in InnoDB). Each tree node occupies one page. Fewer pages read = faster query.
Number of child pointers per internal node. High fan-out keeps tree shallow (3-4 levels for billions of rows).
InnoDB table IS the B+Tree ordered by primary key. Row data lives in leaf pages. Only one per table.
Index that contains all columns needed by a query. Avoids going back to the clustered index (no "bookmark lookup").
When a leaf page is full and a new key must be inserted, the page splits in two. Causes fragmentation over time.

mysql.modes.hash.label: Hash Index
mysql.modes.hash.desc: Hash function maps key to bucket for O(1) lookup. Only supports equality queries (=). Range queries (>, <, BETWEEN) cannot use hash indexes.
mysql.modes.hash.principles: Hash index maps key through a hash function directly to a bucket for O(1) average-case equality lookups
Only supports equality predicates (=, IN); cannot be used for range queries, ORDER BY, or partial matches
Hash collisions are resolved by chaining entries within the same bucket
In MySQL, only the MEMORY engine supports explicit hash indexes; InnoDB uses adaptive hash index internally
Adaptive Hash Index (AHI) in InnoDB is built automatically for hot pages — not manually controlled

mysql.modes.hash.concepts.definition: Maps an arbitrary key to a fixed-size bucket number. Good functions distribute keys uniformly across buckets.
A slot in the hash table. Contains one or more entries (via chaining) that share the same hash value.
Two different keys hash to the same bucket. Resolved by chaining or open addressing.
InnoDB automatically builds an in-memory hash index for frequently accessed B-Tree leaf pages. Transparent to the user.
Constant-time access regardless of table size — the key advantage of hash indexes for point queries.

mysql.modes.composite.label: Composite Index
mysql.modes.composite.desc: Multi-column index follows the leftmost prefix rule. INDEX(a, b, c) can be used for queries on (a), (a,b), or (a,b,c), but NOT (b,c) alone.
mysql.modes.composite.principles: Composite index INDEX(a, b, c) follows the leftmost prefix rule: usable for (a), (a,b), or (a,b,c) but NOT (b,c) alone
Column order matters: place high-selectivity columns and equality predicates first for best filtering
A range predicate on column N stops the index from being used for columns N+1, N+2, etc.
Composite indexes can satisfy ORDER BY if the sort order matches the index column order
The ESR rule (Equality, Sort, Range) guides optimal column ordering in composite indexes

mysql.modes.composite.concepts.definition: Index (a,b,c) can satisfy queries on (a), (a,b), (a,b,c), but not (b), (c), or (b,c). Must start from the left.
Ratio of distinct values to total rows. High selectivity (many unique values) means better filtering power.
MySQL 8.0+ optimization: can use a composite index even when the leading column is skipped, if it has few distinct values.
Equality columns first, then Sort columns, then Range columns — the optimal order for composite index design.
A composite index that includes all columns referenced by the query, eliminating the need for table lookups.

mysql.modes.fulltext.label: Full-Text Index
mysql.modes.fulltext.desc: Inverted index: text is tokenized into words, each word maps to a posting list of document IDs. Supports MATCH...AGAINST queries.
mysql.modes.fulltext.principles: Full-text index creates an inverted index: each word maps to a posting list of document IDs where it appears
Text is tokenized by the parser, then filtered (stopwords removed, minimum word length applied)
Supports three search modes: Natural Language (relevance ranking), Boolean (operators +, -, *), and Query Expansion
InnoDB full-text uses an auxiliary table design with FTS_DOC_ID for document tracking
Full-text search calculates relevance scores based on TF-IDF (Term Frequency - Inverse Document Frequency)

mysql.modes.fulltext.concepts.definition: Maps each unique word to the list of documents containing it. The core data structure behind full-text search.
For each word, stores the list of document IDs (and positions) where that word occurs.
Common words (the, is, at) excluded from indexing because they appear in too many documents to be useful.
Term Frequency * Inverse Document Frequency. Words that are common in a doc but rare overall get higher relevance.
Allows operators: + (must include), - (must exclude), * (wildcard), "" (exact phrase) for precise search control.

mysql.modes.explain.label: EXPLAIN Plan
mysql.modes.explain.desc: Query → Parser → Optimizer → Index Selection → Access Method. EXPLAIN shows how MySQL plans to execute your query, including cost estimates.
mysql.modes.explain.principles: EXPLAIN shows the execution plan the optimizer chose — which indexes, join order, and access methods will be used
The optimizer evaluates multiple plans and picks the one with lowest estimated cost (based on statistics)
Key columns to check: type (access method), key (chosen index), rows (estimated rows to examine), Extra (additional info)
Access types from best to worst: system > const > eq_ref > ref > range > index > ALL
EXPLAIN ANALYZE (MySQL 8.0.18+) actually executes the query and shows real vs estimated row counts

mysql.modes.explain.concepts.definition: How MySQL accesses the table: ALL (full scan), index (full index scan), range (index range), ref (index lookup), const (single row).
Optimizer assigns a cost to each plan based on I/O and CPU. Lower cost = preferred plan. Based on table statistics.
Optimizer picks the index that filters the most rows. May choose full scan if index selectivity is poor.
Appears in Extra when MySQL must sort results outside the index. Often indicates a missing or mismatched index.
Appears in Extra when the query is satisfied entirely from the index (covering index) — no table data access needed.

postgresql.name: PostgreSQL
postgresql.modes.btree.label: B-Tree
postgresql.modes.btree.desc: PostgreSQL B-Tree with MVCC visibility. Each tuple has xmin/xmax transaction IDs. Dead tuples from old transactions remain until VACUUM.
postgresql.modes.btree.principles: PostgreSQL B-Tree stores heap TIDs (tuple identifiers) in leaf nodes, not the actual row data
MVCC means each tuple carries xmin/xmax transaction IDs — visibility is checked at read time, not write time
Dead tuples (from UPDATE/DELETE) remain in index pages until VACUUM removes them
HOT (Heap-Only Tuple) updates avoid index modification when indexed columns are unchanged
Deduplication (v13+) compresses duplicate key entries in leaf pages, reducing index size significantly

postgresql.modes.btree.concepts.definition: Tuple Identifier (block number + offset). Points from an index leaf entry to the actual row in the heap table.
Multi-Version Concurrency Control. Readers never block writers. Each transaction sees a consistent snapshot of data.
Reclaims space from dead tuples. Without it, tables and indexes bloat. Autovacuum runs periodically.
Bitmap tracking which heap pages have only visible-to-all tuples. Enables index-only scans to skip heap lookups.
Heap-Only Tuple update. When no indexed column changes, the new tuple is placed on the same page — no index update needed.

postgresql.modes.hash.label: Hash Index
postgresql.modes.hash.desc: PostgreSQL Hash Index uses a 4-page structure: meta page, bucket pages, overflow pages, bitmap pages. O(1) lookup for equality only.
postgresql.modes.hash.principles: PostgreSQL hash index uses a 4-page structure: meta page, primary buckets, overflow pages, and bitmap pages
Supports only equality comparisons (=); cannot do range, sorting, or multicolumn operations
Since PostgreSQL 10, hash indexes are WAL-logged and crash-safe (previously they were not)
Bucket count doubles when load factor exceeds threshold — split is done incrementally
Hash indexes are smaller than B-Trees for simple equality-only lookups on large text columns

postgresql.modes.hash.concepts.definition: Page 0 of the hash index. Stores bucket count, high mask, low mask, and other control information.
Primary storage for hash entries. Each bucket holds tuples whose hash value maps to that bucket number.
Chained to a bucket when it runs out of space. Forms a linked list from the primary bucket page.
Tracks which overflow pages are currently in use. Enables efficient allocation and deallocation.
When load factor exceeds threshold, one bucket is split into two. Tuples are rehashed to the new bucket.

postgresql.modes.gin.label: GIN Index
postgresql.modes.gin.desc: Generalized Inverted Index for JSONB, arrays, and full-text search. Maps each key/element to a posting list of heap TIDs.
postgresql.modes.gin.principles: GIN (Generalized Inverted Index) maps each element/key to a sorted list of heap TIDs (posting list)
Ideal for multi-valued data types: arrays, JSONB, tsvector (full-text search), hstore
The entry tree is a B-Tree of keys; each key points to a posting tree or compressed posting list
Pending list + fastupdate: new entries are batched in a pending list and merged into the main index later
GIN supports intersection and union of posting lists for multi-key queries (AND/OR)

postgresql.modes.gin.concepts.definition: B-Tree of extracted keys (words, array elements, JSONB paths). Each leaf points to a posting list or posting tree.
Sorted array of heap TIDs for a given key. Stored inline in the entry leaf if small enough.
When a posting list grows too large, it becomes a separate B-Tree of TIDs for efficient lookup and update.
New tuples go to a pending list (unsorted). Periodically merged into the main GIN structure during VACUUM or when list is full.
Operator-class function that checks whether a given key satisfies the query. Used during index scan to filter results.

postgresql.modes.gist.label: GiST Index
postgresql.modes.gist.desc: Generalized Search Tree for geometric and range data. Uses bounding boxes in a tree structure. Supports contains, overlaps, nearest-neighbor.
postgresql.modes.gist.principles: GiST (Generalized Search Tree) is a balanced tree where each entry represents a bounding region (predicate)
Supports nearest-neighbor, containment, overlap, and intersection queries on geometric/range data
Internal nodes store bounding boxes that encompass all child entries — search prunes branches that don't overlap the query
Extensible: custom data types can define their own consistent, union, penalty, and picksplit methods
GiST indexes support exclusion constraints (e.g., no overlapping time ranges in a scheduling table)

postgresql.modes.gist.concepts.definition: Minimum enclosing region for a set of entries. Internal nodes use bounding boxes to decide which subtrees to search.
The most common GiST specialization. Stores spatial data with rectangular bounding boxes for 2D points and polygons.
Function that estimates the cost of inserting an entry into a subtree. GiST picks the subtree with lowest penalty.
Determines how to split an overfull page into two groups. Good splits minimize bounding box overlap.
K-Nearest-Neighbor query. GiST traverses the tree by distance priority, returning closest items first.

postgresql.modes.brin.label: BRIN Index
postgresql.modes.brin.desc: Block Range Index stores min/max per block range. Extremely small index, best for naturally ordered (correlated) data like timestamps.
postgresql.modes.brin.principles: BRIN stores summary data (min/max) for ranges of physical table blocks — extremely small index
Works best when data is naturally ordered on disk (correlated): timestamps, serial IDs, append-only tables
pages_per_range parameter controls granularity: smaller = more precise but larger index
BRIN cannot pinpoint exact rows — it identifies candidate block ranges that must be rechecked
For uncorrelated data, BRIN is useless because every range will contain the full value spectrum

postgresql.modes.brin.concepts.definition: Group of consecutive heap pages (default: 128). BRIN stores one summary entry per range.
Stores min_value and max_value for all tuples in the block range. Used to decide if the range might contain matches.
Statistical measure of how well physical row order matches logical value order. BRIN needs high correlation to be effective.
Reverse mapping structure that maps a block range number to its summary tuple. Enables fast range-to-summary lookup.
BRIN identifies candidate ranges but cannot confirm matches. The executor must recheck actual rows in matching ranges.

mongodb.name: MongoDB
mongodb.modes.single.label: Single Field
mongodb.modes.single.desc: B-Tree index on a single document field. Supports equality, range, and sort queries. MongoDB automatically creates an index on _id.
mongodb.modes.single.principles: MongoDB uses B-Tree indexes on individual document fields for efficient equality, range, and sort queries
Every collection automatically has a unique index on the _id field — this cannot be dropped
Index entries store the field value and a pointer to the document location on disk (RecordId)
Queries that match the index order can avoid an in-memory sort (index provides natural ordering)
Sparse indexes skip documents where the indexed field is missing, saving space and improving selectivity

mongodb.modes.single.concepts.definition: Internal document locator. The index leaf stores field value + RecordId to find the document on disk.
How many distinct values exist relative to total documents. High selectivity = better index performance.
Only indexes documents that contain the indexed field. Useful for optional fields to reduce index size.
Query reads from the index in order. If the query needs only indexed fields, it becomes a covered query (no document fetch).
Full scan of every document when no suitable index exists. Shown as COLLSCAN in explain output.

mongodb.modes.compound.label: Compound Index
mongodb.modes.compound.desc: Multi-key compound index with prefix rule and sort optimization. Index {a:1, b:1, c:1} supports queries on (a), (a,b), (a,b,c).
mongodb.modes.compound.principles: Compound index {a:1, b:1, c:1} supports prefix queries: (a), (a,b), (a,b,c), but NOT (b,c) alone
The ESR rule (Equality, Sort, Range) determines optimal field order in compound indexes
Sort direction in the index matters: {a:1, b:-1} can serve sort({a:1, b:-1}) but NOT sort({a:1, b:1})
A compound index can satisfy queries and sorts simultaneously if field order and directions align
Index intersection (using multiple single-field indexes) is generally slower than a well-designed compound index

mongodb.modes.compound.concepts.definition: Only the leftmost fields of a compound index can be used. {a,b,c} supports queries on (a), (a,b), (a,b,c).
Place Equality fields first, then Sort fields, then Range fields for optimal compound index performance.
Index {a:1, b:-1} sorts a ascending, b descending. The index can serve the exact or fully reversed direction.
The range of index keys to scan. Tighter bounds = fewer keys examined = faster query.
Query whose projection only includes indexed fields. MongoDB returns results directly from the index without fetching documents.

mongodb.modes.multikey.label: Multikey Index
mongodb.modes.multikey.desc: When indexed field contains an array, MongoDB creates separate index entries for each element. One document → multiple index entries.
mongodb.modes.multikey.principles: When an indexed field contains an array, MongoDB creates separate index entries for each array element
One document with N array elements produces N index entries — the index can be significantly larger than the collection
A compound index can have at most one array field (multikey); two array fields in the same compound index are not allowed
Multikey indexes cannot be used as shard keys and have restrictions on covered queries
MongoDB tracks which fields are multikey in the index metadata to adjust query planning

mongodb.modes.multikey.concepts.definition: Each element of the array field gets its own index entry. A document with tags:[a,b,c] creates 3 index entries.
Index metadata marking which fields are multikey. Affects query planner decisions and index bounds calculation.
Compound index where one field is an array. At most one field can be multikey — two array fields is prohibited.
Query operator for matching array elements. With multikey indexes, ensures conditions are applied to the same array element.
Multikey indexes can grow much larger than the collection due to entry-per-element expansion. Monitor with collStats.

mongodb.modes.text.label: Text Index
mongodb.modes.text.desc: Text index with stemming, stop word removal, and TF-IDF scoring. Supports $text queries with $search, $language, $caseSensitive.
mongodb.modes.text.principles: Text index tokenizes string fields into stems, removes stopwords, and builds an inverted index of terms
Each document gets a relevance score based on term frequency and inverse document frequency (TF-IDF)
A collection can have at most one text index, but it can cover multiple string fields with different weights
Text search supports language-specific stemming (e.g., "running" matches "run") and stopword lists
The $text query operator with $meta: "textScore" returns and sorts results by relevance score

mongodb.modes.text.concepts.definition: Reduces words to their root form: "running" -> "run", "connections" -> "connect". Language-specific rules are applied.
Common words excluded from indexing (the, and, is). Language-specific lists. Can be customized per index.
Each field in a compound text index can have a weight (default 1). Higher weight increases that field's impact on relevance scoring.
TF-IDF based relevance score. Access via $meta: "textScore" in projection. Higher score = better match.
$** text index covers all string fields in the document. Useful for collections with dynamic or unknown schema.

redis.name: Redis
redis.modes.zset.label: Sorted Set (ZSET)
redis.modes.zset.desc: Skip list + hash table. ZADD inserts with score, ZRANGE returns by rank, ZRANGEBYSCORE by score range. O(log n) insert/lookup.
redis.modes.zset.principles: Sorted Set combines a skip list (for ordered access) with a hash table (for O(1) member lookup)
Skip list provides O(log n) insert, delete, and range operations with probabilistic balancing
Each element has a unique member name and a floating-point score used for ordering
Elements with equal scores are sorted lexicographically by member name
ZRANGEBYSCORE and ZRANGEBYLEX enable efficient range queries on scores and member names respectively

redis.modes.zset.concepts.definition: Probabilistic data structure with multiple levels of linked lists. Higher levels skip over elements for faster traversal — like an express lane.
Floating-point value (double) that determines element ordering. Multiple members can share the same score.
Each skip list node has a random height (1-32). Higher levels contain fewer nodes, enabling O(log n) search.
Adds member with score. If member exists, updates the score. O(log n) due to skip list repositioning.
For small sorted sets (<128 elements, <64 bytes per entry), Redis uses a compact ziplist (listpack since Redis 7.0) instead of a skip list.

redis.modes.hash-index.label: Hash (Rehashing)
redis.modes.hash-index.desc: Progressive rehashing: Redis uses two hash tables. During resize, entries migrate bucket-by-bucket from ht[0] to ht[1] on each operation.
redis.modes.hash-index.principles: Redis hash tables use progressive (incremental) rehashing — migration happens gradually, not all at once
Two hash tables (ht[0] and ht[1]) coexist during rehashing; every operation migrates one bucket
Rehashing triggers when load factor exceeds 1 (or 5 during BGSAVE) or drops below 0.1
SipHash is used as the hash function since Redis 4.0 (previously MurmurHash2) for distribution and hash-flooding protection
Hash tables grow by doubling and shrink by halving the bucket count

redis.modes.hash-index.concepts.definition: Instead of rehashing all keys at once (which would block), Redis migrates one bucket per operation until complete.
Ratio of used entries to bucket count. Rehashing starts when this exceeds the threshold (1 normally, 5 during BGSAVE).
During rehashing, ht[0] is the old table, ht[1] is the new (larger/smaller) table. Lookups check both.
Each hash table slot is a bucket that can hold a chain of entries with the same hash value.
During background save, Redis delays rehashing (threshold rises to 5) to avoid copy-on-write memory amplification.

redis.modes.secondary.label: Secondary Index
redis.modes.secondary.desc: Redis has no built-in secondary indexes. Pattern: use Sorted Sets as indexes — ZRANGEBYSCORE for numeric ranges, ZRANGEBYLEX for string prefixes.
redis.modes.secondary.principles: Redis has no built-in secondary indexes — they must be implemented using Sorted Sets or Sets as manual indexes
Pattern: ZADD index:field score member to create a numeric index; ZRANGEBYSCORE to query it
For string prefix search, use ZRANGEBYLEX with a lexicographic Sorted Set (all scores = 0)
Application code is responsible for keeping secondary indexes in sync with primary data (no automatic updates)
RediSearch module provides automatic secondary indexing with full-text search capabilities

redis.modes.secondary.concepts.definition: A Sorted Set or Set maintained by application code alongside the primary data. Must be updated on every write.
Queries a Sorted Set by score range. Used to implement numeric range queries on secondary indexes.
Queries a Sorted Set lexicographically (all scores must be equal). Enables string prefix and range searches.
Pattern: encode multiple fields into the member name (e.g., "NYC:Alice") for multi-field secondary lookups.
Redis module providing real-time secondary indexing, full-text search, aggregation, and auto-complete out of the box.
